explanations: The past tense in Pali is formed by adding specific endings to the verb base. For verbs whose bases end in -a, the past tense endings are added directly to the base. An optional augment 'a-' may be prefixed to the verb form to denote the past tense; this 'a-' is not a negative prefix.

Conjugation of verbs with the base ending in -a (e.g., paca = to cook): Singular: 3rd person (So) apaci / paci = He cooked; 2nd person (Tvaṃ) apaci / paci = You cooked; 1st person (Ahaṃ) apaciṃ / paciṃ = I cooked. Plural: 3rd person (Te) apaciṃsu / paciṃsu = They cooked; 2nd person (Tumhe) apacittha / pacittha = You cooked; 1st person (Mayaṃ) apacimha / pacimha = We cooked.
Verbs whose bases end in -nā are also conjugated in the past tense in the same way as bases ending in -a.

Conjugation of verbs with the base ending in -e (e.g., core = to steal): Singular: 3rd person (So) coresi / corayi = He stole; 2nd person (Tvaṃ) coresi = You stole; 1st person (Ahaṃ) coresiṃ / corayiṃ = I stole. Plural: 3rd person (Te) coresuṃ / corayiṃsu = They stole; 2nd person (Tumhe) corayitha = You stole; 1st person (Mayaṃ) corayimha = We stole.

tables.title: Past tense conjugation: base ending in -a (paca = to cook)
Past tense conjugation: base ending in -e (core = to steal)

examples.english: The king wandered in the island.
